description: Asgard (Old Norse: Ásgarðr, "enclosure of the Æsir") is the celestial realm of the Aesir gods in Norse mythology. It sits at the top of Yggdrasil, the World Tree, and is one of the Nine Realms that make up the Norse cosmos. Unlike the Christian concept of Heaven as an eternal paradise, Asgard is a fortress—a place of strength and preparation for the final battle of Ragnarok.
displayName: Asgard - Realm of the Aesir Gods
name: Norse Cosmology

richMetadata.structure: One of Nine Realms located at the top of Yggdrasil. Asgard itself is a fortress containing Aesir halls including Gladsheim, Idavoll plain, and Valhalla. Connected to other realms via Bifrost (rainbow bridge) and Yggdrasil's trunk.
connections: Helheim - realm of the dead (Hel's domain)
Bifrost rainbow bridge - connection between realms
richMetadata.significance: Asgard represents the eternal struggle against entropy and chaos. Unlike heavens in other traditions, Asgard is not eternal—it will fall during Ragnarok. This reflects the Norse view that even gods must face inevitable fate, emphasizing honor and brave action over salvation.
parallels: Hindu Indraloka - celestial realm of gods
Celtic Tír na nÓg - divine otherworld

keyMyths.title: The Building of Asgard's Walls
keyMyths.description: After the Aesir established themselves, a giant offered to build impenetrable walls around Asgard in exchange for the sun, moon, and the hand of Freya. The gods, with Loki's 'help', tricked the giant, leading to his death and the completion of the wall.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Yggdrasil
keyMyths.description: Yggdrasil, the World Tree, connects the Nine Realms of Norse cosmology. Its roots extend into Hel, Jotunheim, and Asgard, nourished by sacred wells. It is a symbol of the interconnectedness and vital force of the cosmos.
keyMyths.source: Gylfaginning, Prose Edda; Grímnismál, Poetic Edda

keyMyths.title: The Bifrost Bridge
keyMyths.description: Bifrost, the rainbow bridge, connects Asgard to Midgard (the realm of humans). It is guarded by Heimdall, whose horn will signal the beginning of Ragnarok. The bridge is destined to break during the final battle.

keyMyths.title: Odin's All-Seeing Throne (Hlidskjalf)
keyMyths.description: Odin sits upon Hlidskjalf, his high seat in Asgard, which allows him to see into all Nine Realms. From this vantage point, he gathers knowledge and oversees the workings of the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: The Mead of Poetry
keyMyths.description: The Mead of Poetry, created from the blood of Kvasir, grants wisdom and poetic inspiration to those who drink it. It was initially guarded by giants but stolen by Odin, who brought it to Asgard to share with the gods and worthy humans.

sources.name: The Poetic Edda
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, primarily preserved in the Codex Regius manuscript, dealing with mythological and heroic subjects.
sources.title: The Poetic Edda

sources.name: The Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson)
sources.description: A textbook of Old Norse poetics, containing many myths and legends, written in the 13th century by Snorri Sturluson.
sources.title: The Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson)

sources.name: Gesta Danorum (Saxo Grammaticus)
sources.description: A patriotic work of Danish history, written in Latin by Saxo Grammaticus in the late 12th and early 13th centuries. It contains euhemerized versions of Norse myths.
sources.title: Gesta Danorum (Saxo Grammaticus)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Viking Age
sources.description: Runestones, bracteates, and other artifacts that depict mythological scenes and provide insight into Norse beliefs.
sources.title: Archaeological evidence from the Viking Age

sources.name: Lokasenna (Poetic Edda)
sources.description: A poem from the Poetic Edda that provides insight into the relationships and rivalries between the gods in Asgard.
sources.title: Lokasenna (Poetic Edda)
